fuzzy_prd:
We need a front-end component that takes raw JavaScript or TypeScript source text and tells us what happened when we tried to understand it. The caller passes a chunk of code and a few flags about what dialect it might be — whether it uses type annotations, whether it might be a declaration file, whether it has JSX syntax, and whether it should be treated as a module or a classic script. The component then hands back a structured summary: did the parse completely fall over, how many top-level constructs are in the program, how many of those leading string literals count as directives (like 'use strict'), how many comments were pulled out as trivia, what kind each comment is, how many problems were found, and what the problem messages say. If the dialect is something we don't support — handle it the same way the rest of the diagnostics pipeline handles unsupported features — with a clear stable message rather than producing misleading output. The output should be a fixed line-oriented report so any downstream tool can read it without parsing JSON.

A couple more specifics from the questions that came in: the request needs a required 'source' field, plus four optional boolean flags that all default to false: 'typescript' (accept type-annotation syntax), 'typescript_definition' (treat as type-declaration file), 'jsx' (accept JSX), and 'module' (parse as module; false means script).

Also, the report shape should be completely fixed. It is exactly these lines in this order, each terminated by a newline: panicked=<true|false>, body=<n>, directives=<n>, comments=<n>, comment_kind=<line|block> (one per comment, omitted entirely when comments=0), errors=<n>, error=<message> (one per diagnostic, omitted entirely when errors=0).

One special case to be explicit about: the exact error message is 'Flow is not supported'. When the flow pragma is detected and parsing fails, panicked=true, body=0, directives=0, comments=1 (the pragma is counted as a comment), comment_kind matches the pragma style (line or block), errors=1, error=Flow is not supported.

trigger_keywords: when is a string literal counted as a directive vs an expression
what makes a string literal a prologue directive
does a string after import count as a directive
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A string literal is a prologue directive only when it appears at the very start of the program before any other construct. If it follows an import declaration, export declaration, or decorator, it is parsed as an ordinary expression statement and directives=0. A leading import + trailing string literal gives body=2, directives=0. A decorator applied to the string literal gives body=1, directives=0.

trigger_keywords: what are the two comment kind values
how are single-line versus block comments distinguished
what value is reported for a double-slash comment vs a slash-star comment
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: comment_kind=line for a single-line comment (introduced by //). comment_kind=block for a delimited comment (/* ... */). One comment_kind line is emitted per comment in source order. The comment_kind section is omitted entirely when comments=0.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handle it the same way the rest of the diagnostics pipeline handles unsupported features
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The flow-dialect check is implemented in the oxc_parser crate. When the source begins with a flow pragma (// @flow or /* @flow */) and parsing subsequently fails, the parser emits exactly one diagnostic with the message 'Flow is not supported' and sets panicked=true.

oracle_data.fallback_response: Please consult the start.md specification for the exact output contract. The report always contains the lines: panicked, body, directives, comments, optionally comment_kind lines, errors, and optionally error lines — in that fixed order.

feature1_empty_program.json.description: Parse a program whose text is empty or contains only insignificant whitespace (spaces, tabs, line breaks). The parser must succeed without aborting and yield a valid but empty program: no top-level statements, no prologue directives, and no comments. No diagnostics are produced.

feature2_flow_rejection.json.description: Parse JavaScript whose very first line carries a flow type-checker pragma (either a single-line comment pragma or a block comment pragma at the start of the file) followed by code that is not valid plain JavaScript. The parser does not support the flow dialect, so when parsing of such a file fails it aborts with an empty program and reports exactly one diagnostic stating that this dialect is unsupported. The leading pragma is itself a comment and is still counted among the extracted comments, with its kind reflecting whether the pragma was written as a single-line or block comment.

cases.expected_output:
panicked=true
body=0
directives=0
comments=1
comment_kind=line
errors=1
error=Flow is not supported

panicked=true
body=0
directives=0
comments=1
comment_kind=block
errors=1
error=Flow is not supported

feature3_directive_prologue.json.description: A string-literal expression such as 'use strict' is only recognised as a prologue directive when it appears at the very start of a program, before any other construct. When such a string literal instead follows a leading construct (an import declaration, an export declaration, or a decorator), it is no longer in directive position and must be parsed as an ordinary expression statement. In every such case the program reports zero prologue directives, and the leading construct together with the trailing string-literal statement appear as ordinary top-level statements in the body. Parsing succeeds without aborting and produces no diagnostics.

feature4_comment_extraction.json.description: While parsing, comments are extracted as trivia separately from the program's statements. Each extracted comment records its kind: a single-line comment introduced by a double-slash, or a block comment delimited by slash-star and star-slash. A block comment embedded inside an otherwise valid construct is still extracted as one comment and does not affect the surrounding statement. The parser reports the total number of comments and, for each, its kind in source order. Type-annotation syntax is enabled for these inputs.

cases.expected_output:
panicked=false
body=0
directives=0
comments=1
comment_kind=line
errors=0

panicked=false
body=0
directives=0
comments=1
comment_kind=block
errors=0

feature5_expression_statements.json.description: Parse short but valid programs and confirm the parser produces a non-empty program body. A lone BigInt literal forms a single expression statement. A leading empty statement (a bare semicolon) followed by a long numeric-looking string literal forms two top-level statements. In both cases parsing succeeds without aborting, produces no prologue directives and no diagnostics, and the body contains the expected number of statements.
